I love my job.
I am here. Do not worry.
Turn it off.
I need the report by Friday.
We finished the project on time.
I love my team and my job.
I want to go.
A cup of tea.
Turn it off now.
Welcome back, Daniela. Your sounds are much clearer now. Today we work on rhythm. Ready?
Yes. People say I sound a little robotic. I give every word the same force.
That is common. Push the content words and reduce the small ones. Listen: I need the report by Friday.
So I make need, report, and Friday strong, and the rest soft?
Exactly. And use contractions. Say I am instead of I am, and do not instead of do not.
I am ready and I do not agree. It already feels faster and friendlier.
Perfect. One more habit: linking. Join the words: turn it off becomes one smooth phrase.
Turn it off. I will practice the rhythm every day before my presentation.
I am not sure about that.
Do not worry, I will call you.
We are working on it right now.
Let me stress the important words.

Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Last time we worked on single sounds. Today, three quick tips about rhythm, for your big presentation on Friday. First, stress the important words. In a sentence, the nouns, the main verbs, and the adjectives carry the meaning. Say them a little louder and longer. The small words, like a, the, to, and of, stay short and soft. Second, use contractions. Say I am as I'm, do not as don't, and we will as we'll. Contractions sound natural and friendly, not lazy. Third, let your words flow together. Do not stop after every word like a robot. Link the end of one word to the start of the next, so turn it off sounds like one smooth phrase. You do not need to speak fast. You need a clear, natural rhythm. Practice these three things for ten minutes a day, and on Friday you will sound calm and confident. You can do it. Talk soon.

Hey Daniela, it is Bruno from the Lisbon office. I heard you are working on your rhythm, so let me tell you my story. For a long time, I said every word with the same strong force. I love this project came out as I. Love. This. Project. It sounded like a robot, and people had to listen very hard. Then a colleague gave me one simple tip. Relax the small words, and push the big ones. So I started to say the important words clearly and let the little words almost disappear. I also began to use contractions, like I'm and we're, instead of the full forms. Suddenly people said my English sounded much more natural. This week I am working on linking, joining words together so they flow. By the way, your English already has a nice rhythm. Keep going, and I will see you at the team meeting on Thursday.

The important words that carry meaning are content words.
Small grammar words like a and to are function words.
I am becomes the contraction I'm.
Joining the end of one word to the next is called linking.
We reduce the small words to keep the rhythm.
Good rhythm gives your speech a natural flow.
I need the report by Friday.
I am not sure about that.
Do not worry, I will call you.
We are working on it right now.
Let me stress the important words.
